methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Climate & Energy (found 2 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 21 same-sector peers exist in Climate & Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: QuantumScape
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Public), post-money $5B+

private.name: LanzaTech
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Public), post-money $800M+

data.narrative:
Brimstone operates in Climate & Energy as a public-stage company that has raised $100M+. The comparable universe within Climate & Energy comprises 21 same-sector peers overall, including 1 public company (LanzaTech, ticker LNZA) and 8 private companies. Top private peers identified include QuantumScape, TAE Technologies, Redwood Materials, and Helion.

Peer-median valuation methodology is not applicable to this analysis. The narrow peer set—defined by same stage, adjacent stage, or similar valuation tier—yielded only 2 same-stage comparables, falling short of the 3-datapoint minimum required for reliable triangulation. Expanding to all 21 same-sector peers would introduce material methodological error by mixing heterogeneous stages and business models. No adjacent-stage or similar-tier peers met inclusion thresholds.

The absence of sufficient narrow peers creates a significant constraint on traditional comparable-company analysis for Brimstone. The single public comp (LNZA) and the identified private peer set provide reference points for sector dynamics but cannot support quantitative valuation guidance in isolation. Practitioners should rely on alternative methods—such as discounted cash flow modeling or venture-backed precedent pricing—to establish a defensible range. Key uncertainties include the applicability of any single public comparable and the heterogeneity of private peer trajectories in an early-stage, capital-intensive sector.
